Anna never imagined that the worst day of her life would begin with a phone call. It was a Tuesday morning, and she was at work when her phone rang. The voice on the other end was calm but urgent. There had been an accident. Her son Ethan, who was eight years old, had been rushed to the hospital. Anna's world stopped. She dropped everything and ran to her car, her hands shaking so badly she could barely hold the steering wheel. When she arrived at the hospital, doctors were waiting for her. They told her that Ethan had been hit by a car while crossing the street after school. He had died instantly. The words didn't make sense at first. Died? Her son? No, that couldn't be right. Ethan was supposed to come home that afternoon. He was supposed to do his homework. He was supposed to grow up, go to college, have children of his own. He was supposed to bury her, not the other way around. But six months later, Anna found something that would change everything. In Ethan's room, she discovered a notebook with a list he had written. At the top it said "Things I Want to Do to Help People." And Anna realized that maybe, just maybe, she could do those things for him.
